one_line_takeaway: A modular perception-plus-LLM pipeline breaks six commercial Visual Reasoning CAPTCHA providers at up to 98.5% accuracy — matching humans — and shows current VRCs' security rests on brittle language-vision alignments that even simple prompt-wording randomization only partly defends.
key_finding: VIPER (GPT-4o backend, R3) sets state-of-the-art per-platform accuracy: VTT 97.33%, Geetest 86.45%, NetEase 92.61%, DingXiang 91.48%, Shumei 98.45%, Xiaodun 92.59% (Table 3), matching or exceeding the human baseline on several platforms (e.g., VTT humans 87.60%, Xiaodun humans 91.80%) and far surpassing the strongest LLM prior solver Oedipus (VTT 70.23%). Ablation shows the design is essential: raw LLM-only (R1) scores only 7.1%–31.4%, adding the detector (R2) lifts every platform above 60%, and the full pipeline (R3) reaches 86.5%–98.5%. The attack stays above ~82% across all four LLM backends. The proposed TSR defense meaningfully but incompletely degrades solvers: pure linguistic perturbation (synonyms + relation rewording + indirection, S+R+I) drops VIPER from 93.60% to 85.20%, Oedipus from 65.80% to 45.30%, and GraphNet from 83.20% to 68.40%, showing solvers rely on brittle language-vision alignments rather than deep semantics.
core_contribution: VIPER (Visual Inference via Perception and Embedded Reasoning) is an attack framework that breaks Visual Reasoning CAPTCHAs (VRCs) by decoupling structured visual perception from LLM-based reasoning. Its own pipeline (§4) has three modules: (1) Exterior, which ingests the challenge image I and natural-language prompt q; (2) Viewer, a perception backbone built on a single YOLOv11-style multi-object detector that emits compound SHAPE×COLOR×ORIENTATION labels (91-category ontology), a Question Information Extractor (QIE) that parses q into attribute slots, an Integrator that filters detections to semantically relevant candidates, and a Relative Position Information Extractor (RPIE) that resolves spatial-relation targets via geometric projection (Algorithm 1); and (3) Policymaker, which classifies the reasoning type (attribute/spatial/comparative), builds a task-conditioned prompt over the pruned candidate set, and queries an LLM (single pass, no chain-of-thought) to emit one click coordinate (x,y). Secondary contributions: a 6,000-challenge cross-platform benchmark plus a 1,200-image annotated detector dataset, and a defense proposal, Template-Space Randomization (TSR).
threat_model: Explicitly defined in §3. Attacker is an external adversary with NO privileged access to the target service's infrastructure, proprietary CAPTCHA-generation logic, or private training data; interaction is strictly BLACK-BOX through public interfaces (e.g., web auth portals) under the same session/network constraints as legitimate users. The adversary receives only binary feedback on submission correctness — no gradients, intermediate model states, or control over challenge construction — but may collect unlimited VRC instances within normal usage limits, capture all rendered images and prompts, and apply arbitrary compute (pretrained LMMs, custom perception modules, symbolic reasoning). Goal: solve VRCs at a rate that undermines the human-exclusive verification guarantee. Explicitly OUT of scope: software-vulnerability exploitation, backend compromise, side-channel exfiltration. Defender is the service operator controlling challenge generation, rate limits, and server-side verification; all challenge content is assumed visible to the client (hence the adversary).
limitations: Author-stated (§7): (1) only advanced LLMs were tested — no small/lightweight backbones — so VIPER's modular design cannot be cleanly disentangled from the raw reasoning power of large models; (2) no systematic computational-cost/API-cost analysis despite the multi-stage LLM pipeline; (3) scope limited to VRCs — results do not generalize to behavioral or interaction-based CAPTCHAs (e.g., reCAPTCHA v3); (4) the TSR defense was evaluated only against automated solvers with NO human-subject study, so its usability and cognitive-load impact on legitimate users is unverified, and its evaluation is explicitly 'preliminary' at 600 queries. Reviewer-noted: baselines were re-implemented by the authors rather than run from original code, and RPIE's fixed-offset geometric projection is a hand-tuned heuristic whose sensitivity is not characterized.

datasets_used: Unified VRC benchmark: 6,000 evaluation challenges (1,000 uniformly sampled images per provider) across six commercial providers
VTT (Tencent Waterproof Wall / Visual Turing Test): 12,000 crawled via Selenium
Detector training set: 1,200 annotated multi-object VRC images (200 per provider; 800 train / 200 val / 200 test)
TSR defense set: 600 queries (100 original images × 6 linguistic variants: S, R, I, S+R, S+I, S+R+I)
quantitative_results: VIPER R3 (GPT-4o) accuracy: VTT 97.33%, Geetest 86.45%, NetEase 92.61%, DingXiang 91.48%, Shumei 98.45%, Xiaodun 92.59% (Table 3)
Ablation R1 (LLM-only, GPT-4o): 31.36% VTT down to 7.07% Shumei
Ablation R2 (detector→prompt, GPT-4o): all platforms >60%, e.g., VTT 75.79%, Shumei 77.08%
Human baseline: VTT 87.60%, Geetest 90.70%, NetEase 95.12%, DingXiang 95.40%, Shumei 93.20%, Xiaodun 91.80%
Detector (Table 4): precision 0.998, recall 0.956, mAP@50 0.984, mAP@50:95 0.919; APs 0.912 / APm 0.944 / APl 0.963; 25.6M params, 72.4 GFLOPs, 7.8 ms/img (128 img/s), 0.9 ms NMS
Response time: VIPER overall ~8.3s (GPT-4o fastest 5–6s; DeepSeek/Kimi 9–11s; Grok-2 7–9s) vs human baseline 7.0s; Holistic <0.05s, GraphNet ~0.20s, Oedipus ~3s
TSR (Table 6, avg over six platforms): VIPER 93.60%→85.20% (S+R+I); Oedipus 65.80%→45.30%; GraphNet 83.20%→68.40%; Holistic 89.40%→84.90%
Zero-shot GPT-4o context result: VRC accuracy ~31% vs reCAPTCHA ~50% (Appendix Table 7), motivating VRCs as a harder frontier
baselines_compared: Holistic model (BiLSTM + rule-based reasoning; VTT 88.12%, avg 89.5%) — vision/perception-driven
VRC-GraphNet (object/relation graph + GNN; VTT 89.47%, avg 83.2%) — graph-reasoning
Oedipus (LLM + DSL + chain-of-thought; VTT 70.23%, avg 65.8%) — LLM/reasoning-driven
Human baseline (10 participants on the same benchmark corpus)
